questions.question: Explain the XOAR approach to self-protecting software. Describe its core principles and how it improves cloud application security.
keyPoints: XOAR decomposes monolithic applications into isolated, least-privilege compartments to limit the impact of vulnerabilities.
Core principle 1 — Privilege Separation: Break the application into multiple compartments, each handling a specific function (e.g., HTTP parser, CGI engine, SSL handler, logger).
Core principle 2 — Least Privilege: Each compartment receives only the minimum permissions needed for its function.
Each compartment runs as a separate process with its own address space, communicating through controlled interfaces (IPC, message passing).
Benefits: reduced blast radius, smaller attack surface per compartment, easier auditing, independent patching.
XOAR complements VM and OS security by adding application-level defense in depth — even if VM and OS are secure, a monolithic app vulnerability can still lead to compromise.

questions.question: Compare monolithic and XOAR-decomposed application architectures in terms of security.
keyPoints: Monolithic: entire app runs with same privileges — one vulnerability gives attacker full access to all capabilities.
XOAR: each compartment has minimal privileges — compromising one compartment doesn't compromise others.
Monolithic has larger attack surface (entire codebase); XOAR has smaller attack surface (per compartment).
XOAR adds complexity (IPC overhead, more complex architecture) but significantly improves security containment.
